1.17: vayeman YHWH dag gadol livlo et Yonah vayehi Yonah bime'ei haddag shlosha yamim ushlosha leilot: 'And the LORD appointed a great fish to swallow up Jonah. And Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.' The historicity of Jonah has been debated throughout church history — a man surviving inside a large sea creature is extraordinary. Jesus's citation of Jonah in Matt 12:40 takes the narrative as historical: 'just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.' If Jonah is a parable, Jesus is drawing a parable-to-reality comparison, which is unusual. The orthodox reading: both Jonah's experience and Jesus's resurrection are historical events in which the later was the greater antitype of the earlier.
1.1: vayehi davar YHWH el Yonah ben Amittai lemor: 'Now the word of the LORD came to Jonah son of Amittai, saying.' The standard prophetic commissioning formula (davar YHWH) opens a book that is, uniquely, a narrative about a prophet rather than a collection of his oracles. Jonah (yonah, H3124) means 'dove' — an ironic name for a prophet who flies away rather than descending with the divine message. Ben Amittai identifies him with the historical figure of 2 Kgs 14:25 (a prophet under Jeroboam II), grounding this narrative in concrete history.
1.2: qum lech el Nineveh hair hagedolah: 'Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city.' The dual imperative qum lech (arise-go) is the standard commissioning formula for a prophetic mission (cf. 1 Kgs 17:9, Elijah to Zarephath — another Gentile mission). Nineveh (nineveh) was the capital of Assyria — Israel's most feared enemy. Veqera aleiha (cry out against it) uses the verb qara which covers both proclamation and calling out; the reason given: ki aleta ra'atam lepanai — 'for their evil has come up before me.' The phrase ra'ah will form an ironic wordplay with ch4:1 (vayera... raah gedolah — it was evil to Jonah with great evil when God spared the 'evil' Nineveh).
1.3: vayaqam Yonah livroach Tarshishah mipnei YHWH: 'But Jonah rose to flee to Tarshish from the face of the LORD.' Three verbs of descent mark ch1-2 as a structural unit: here Jonah goes down to Joppa (vayered Yafo), then down into the ship (vayered besefina), then down into the ship's interior (vayishkav vayiradam). Tarshish — traditionally identified with Tartessos in Spain — represents the western extreme of the known world, the maximum distance from Nineveh (in the east) and from YHWH's commission. The irony the narrator will develop: you cannot flee from the omnipresent God (cf. Ps 139:9-10).
1.4: vaYHWH hetil ruach gedolah el hayam: 'But the LORD hurled a great wind into the sea.' The verb hetil (H2904, to hurl/cast) is the first of several occurrences of 'casting' in ch1: the sailors cast cargo (v5), they cast lots (v7), Jonah asks to be cast into the sea (v12), they cast him (v15). God's sovereign hurling sets the chain in motion. Sa'ar gadol (great tempest) — the storm is YHWH's agent, not a random weather event; the ship (ha-oniyah) is described as thinking it would be broken (chashevah lehishaver), the verb chashav (to think/plan) usually applied to persons — a brief personification of the crisis.
1.5: vayira'u hamaluachim: 'And the sailors feared.' Three actions follow: they feared, they cried each man to his god (kol ish el elohav — the sailors are pagans worshipping multiple deities), and they cast cargo into the sea to lighten it. Meanwhile: veYonah yarad el yereketi hasefinah vayishkav vayiradam — 'Jonah had gone down into the innermost parts of the ship and was lying there fast asleep.' The descent-verb yarad again; yereketi (sides/extremities — the deep inner hold) intensifies the going-down motif. Jonah's sleep during the storm contrasts with Jesus sleeping during the storm in Matt 8:24 — the same scene, opposite meanings: Jonah sleeps in guilty flight, Jesus sleeps in sovereign peace.
1.6: ma lecha nirdam: 'What are you doing here, sleeping?' The captain's rebuke (rab hachovlim = the chief of the rope-pullers/officers) is theologically charged: qum qera el eloheicha — 'arise, call on your God.' The pagan captain uses the very commissioning language YHWH had spoken to Jonah: qum qera. The captain's inadvertent echo of God's command exposes the irony: a pagan commander is telling the Hebrew prophet to do what God already told him to do. Ulai yitashtet ha'elohim lanu — 'perhaps God will think of us' — the hopeful uncertainty of the pagans contrasts with Jonah's certain knowledge of YHWH.
1.7: lechu venappilah goralot: 'Come, let us cast lots.' The sailors use the lot (goral, H1486) to identify the source of the calamity — standard ancient Near Eastern practice for determining divine causation. Vayipol hagoral al Yonah — 'and the lot fell on Jonah.' The sovereignty of God operates through what appears to be chance; Prov 16:33 states that 'the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD.' The lot is YHWH's chosen instrument to expose his fleeing prophet — even in the mechanism of pagan divination, YHWH's hand is at work.
1.8: haggida lanu bishelo hara'ah hazot lanu: 'Tell us on whose account this evil has come upon us.' The sailors interrogate Jonah with four parallel questions: What is your occupation? Where do you come from? What is your country? Of what people are you? The accumulation of identity questions drives toward the point: who are you in relation to the deity who controls this storm? Bishelo — 'on account of whom' — is an unusual construction marking the causal guilt. Jonah must face what he has been running from: his identity as a servant of YHWH.
1.9: ivri anochi ve'et YHWH elohei hashamayim ani yare: 'I am a Hebrew, and I fear the LORD, the God of heaven.' Jonah's confession is loaded: ivri (Hebrew) identifies him with Israel's covenant identity; yerei YHWH (I fear YHWH) is the language of true piety — but used by a man who is in active flight from YHWH. The title elohei hashamayim (God of heaven) — emphasizing cosmic sovereignty — is precisely the wrong thing to tell the sailors if you want to calm them: this God made the sea and the dry land, so the storm is no accident. The sailors' fear (vayiru) intensifies.
1.10: ki mipnei YHWH hu voreach: 'For he was fleeing from the presence of the LORD.' The narrator's explanatory aside confirms what the sailors now understand: this man's flight from YHWH's presence is the cause of the storm. Mipnei YHWH (from the face/presence of YHWH) recalls v3 — the same phrase used three times in the chapter (vv3, 3, 10). The idiom conveys both literal distance and the covenantal relationship implied: one who stands before a king is in his service; to flee from his face is to abandon that service.
1.11: mah naaseh lach veyishtok hayam me'aleinu: 'What shall we do to you, that the sea may quiet down for us?' The sailors turn to Jonah for the solution — a remarkable reversal: the pagans look to the Hebrew prophet for theological guidance. Yishtoq (be quiet/calm, from shataqa = to be still) — the same verbal root used in Ps 107:29 ('he made the storm be still [yashtoq]') — the sailors' question is answered by Jonah and then by YHWH, in sequence. The sea was going and growing more stormy (holech vesaoer) — the participle construction intensifying the worsening storm.
1.12: qachuni vehashlichuni el hayam veyishtok hayam me'aleichem: 'Pick me up and hurl me into the sea; then the sea will quiet down for you.' Jonah's instruction is the decisive turning point of ch1. He does not volunteer to jump — the verb hashlichuni (throw me) requires the sailors' action. He accepts responsibility: ki yodea anochi ki veshelli hasaar hagadol hazeh aleichem — 'for I know that it is because of me that this great storm has come upon you.' This is Jonah's first honest speech: the acknowledgment of guilt and the substitutionary offer — throw me into the sea so you will be saved. Theologically this is the structure (if not the intent) of substitutionary atonement.
1.13: vayachteru ha'anashim lehashiv el hayabasha: 'Nevertheless, the men rowed hard to get back to dry land.' The verb vayachteru (from chatr = to dig; extended to rowing = digging/cutting through water) occurs only here and Ps 139:8 (where im ashkiv sheol = if I make my bed in Sheol) — an interesting shared semantic field with the underworld theme. The sailors' effort to save Jonah without throwing him overboard demonstrates their moral instinct against bloodshed — but the sea (holech vesaer, going and raging) overwhelms their effort.
1.14: al titen aleinu dam naki: 'Do not put innocent blood on us.' The sailors pray to YHWH for the first time — a significant conversion-echo — using the legal formula of dam naki (innocent blood, H1818 + H5355). Their prayer acknowledges YHWH's sovereign will: ki atta YHWH kasher chafatzta asita — 'for you, O LORD, have done as it pleased you.' This is a theological confession of divine sovereignty — remarkable from pagan sailors. The fear of incurring bloodguilt for killing Jonah shows a moral seriousness that contrasts with Jonah's callousness toward the Ninevites.
1.15: vayise'u et Yonah vayashlichuhu el hayam vayaamod hayam mizzaopo: 'So they picked up Jonah and hurled him into the sea, and the sea ceased from its raging.' The verb vayashlichuhu (and they hurled him) completes the chain of casting begun in v4 (hetil, God hurled the wind). God's original act — hurling the storm — is answered by the sailors' act — hurling Jonah — and the sea's immediate ceasing (vayaamod, stood still) confirms the divine connection. The sea's instant response is the narrative's vindication: the sailors acted correctly, Jonah's word was true, and YHWH's purpose is accomplished through the very storm he sent.
1.16: vayiru ha'anashim yir'ah gedolah et YHWH: 'Then the men feared the LORD exceedingly.' The sailors' response to the calming of the sea is a complete religious transformation: vayir'u... yir'ah gedolah (feared... great fear) — the same phrase used in v5 (vayira'u hamaluachim), but now directed at YHWH rather than the storm. They offer a sacrifice (vayizbechu-zebach) and make vows (vayidderu nedarim) — the language of covenant worship. The Gentile sailors have been converted by the storm; by contrast, the Hebrew prophet required a fish.

2.1: vayitpalel Yonah el YHWH elohav mim'ei haddag: 'Then Jonah prayed to the LORD his God from the belly of the fish.' The prayer of ch2 is a psalm of thanksgiving for deliverance already received — it does not petition for rescue from the fish but celebrates a rescue that Jonah views as having already occurred (when he was saved from drowning). The structural irony: Jonah's prayer is more theologically coherent inside the fish than his behavior had been outside it. Mim'ei haddag (from the belly of the fish) — me'ei literally 'entrails/inward parts' — the most visceral term for the fish's interior, underscoring the extremity of the location.
2.2: qarati mitzara li el YHWH vaya'aneni: 'I called out to the LORD out of my distress, and he answered me.' The psalm follows the standard lament-to-praise structure: crisis (tzara, distress) → cry to YHWH → divine response → deliverance. Mibeten sheol shivati — 'out of the belly of Sheol I cried' — the fish's belly is identified as Sheol (the underworld/realm of the dead), not a literal description but the experiential intensity of near-death and hopelessness. This identification is the basis for Jesus's typological reading: 'three days and three nights in the heart of the earth' (Matt 12:40) corresponds to Jonah's Sheol experience.
2.3: vatashlicheni metzulah bilevav yamim: 'You cast me into the deep, into the heart of the seas.' Jonah addresses YHWH directly as the agent of his trial — a bold theological move. The verb is vatashlicheni (you cast me) — the same root as the sailors' hashlichuni and God's hetil: the whole narrative is structured around this 'casting' motif. Metzulah (the deep, H4688) and levav yamim (heart of the seas) are the language of chaotic waters — the domain of death from which only YHWH can rescue. Mishebarecha vegalecha avari — 'all your waves and billows passed over me' — using possessive: YHWH's waves, not random forces.
2.4: va'ani amarti nigzarti mineged eineicha: 'Then I said, I am driven away from your sight.' The word nigzarti (I am cut off, H1644) carries the weight of exile — the same root used in Lam 3:54 and Ps 88:5 for being 'cut off' from divine care. This is Jonah's lament at its deepest: not physical death but covenantal separation from YHWH's face. The pivot follows immediately: ach osif lehabit el hekhal qodshecha — 'yet I shall again look toward your holy temple' — the turn from despair to hope. The temple (heikhal) is the point of YHWH's accessible presence; looking toward it is the posture of prayer (1 Kgs 8:48; Dan 6:10).
2.5: afafuni mayim ad nefesh: 'The waters closed in over me to my very life.' Three images of overwhelming: waters (mayim) to the nefesh (life/throat); the deep (tehom) surrounded him; suf chavush lero'shi — 'weeds wrapped around my head.' Suf (H5488) is the same word as in yam suf (Red Sea/Sea of Reeds): the plant that grows in the water of the Exodus crossing has become the thing that entangles in the water of near-death. The Exodus lexicon is inverted: the sea of deliverance is the sea of drowning, until YHWH reverses it again.
2.6: leqitsvot harim yaradti: 'I went down to the roots of the mountains.' The cosmology of the verse: the mountains' roots reach into the watery deep; their foundations (qitsvot, extremities/bases) are the lowest point of the earth. Haeretz beveriacheha baadi leolam — 'the earth with its bars closed over me forever' — the bolts (beriachim) of the earth-gates, the prison-lock of death itself. The stunning reversal: vataal mishachat chayai YHWH elohai — 'yet you brought up my life from the pit, O LORD my God.' Mishachat (pit/corruption, H7845) — the Hebrew word for the grave and decay; YHWH brings up from the lowest point.
2.7: behitatef alai nafshi et YHWH zacharti: 'When my life was fainting away, I remembered the LORD.' The verb hitatef (H5848, to faint/languish — used also in Ps 142:4; 143:4 for the spirit overwhelming within) marks the crisis point from which the turn to YHWH comes. Vatavoqefillati eleicha el hekhal qodshecha — 'and my prayer came to you, into your holy temple.' The word vatavo (and it came) — the prayer as a person traveling to reach YHWH's temple. The direction of prayer reverses the direction of flight: Jonah had fled from YHWH's presence; his prayer now arrives at YHWH's dwelling.
2.8: meshamerim havelei shav chasdham yaazovu: 'Those who pay regard to worthless idols forsake their hope of steadfast love.' This theological aphorism interrupts the autobiographical narrative to make a universal point: havelei shav (worthless vanities — the term hevel is Ecclesiastes' key word for futility; shav = empty/false) are the idols that the pagan sailors of ch1 had been crying to. To cling to them is to abandon (yaazovu) one's chesed — the steadfast covenant love that YHWH gives and that connects his people to him. The sailors had begun to turn away from their havelei shav (v5 → v16); this verse is the theological reason why.
2.9: va'ani beqol todah ezbeach lach: 'But I with the voice of thanksgiving will sacrifice to you.' The emphatic pronoun va'ani (but I/and I) contrasts Jonah with the idol-venerators: he will sacrifice with the voice of thanksgiving. Asher nadarti ashalema — 'what I have vowed I will pay' — vow-fulfillment in the temple was the standard act of thanksgiving for answered prayer (Ps 116:14; Ps 22:25). The closing confession: yeshuah laYHWH — 'salvation belongs to the LORD.' Three words that summarize the theology of the entire book: the nations, the storm, the fish, the worm — all belong to YHWH and all serve his salvation purposes.
2.10: vayomer YHWH ladag vayaqe et Yonah el hayabasha: 'And the LORD spoke to the fish, and it vomited Jonah out on dry land.' The fish obeys YHWH's single command — vayomer YHWH (the LORD spoke) is the creator's speech-act authority. The verb vayaqe (vomited, H6958) is blunt and physical — not a gentle release but an expulsion. El hayabasha (onto the dry land) — the same phrase used in 1:9 ('I fear YHWH, who made the sea and the dry land'): the God who made the dry land now has it receive his prophet from the sea. The descent-motif (ch1: down to Joppa → down into the ship → down into the fish's belly/Sheol) is fully reversed: Jonah is back on dry land.

3.1: vayhi davar YHWH el Yonah shenit lemor: 'Then the word of the LORD came to Jonah the second time, saying.' The phrase shenit (a second time) is one of Scripture's most grace-laden words: YHWH reissues the commission to the fled prophet without reproach. There is no divine rebuke, no added condition, no revision of the mission — the same words as 1:2 follow verbatim. The 'second time' motif in Scripture regularly signals renewal after failure (the second giving of the tablets, Exod 34; Elijah's second angel-touch, 1 Kgs 19:7). YHWH's persistence with failed messengers is the book's first object-lesson in divine patience.
3.2: qum lech el Nineveh hair hagedolah: 'Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city.' The commissioning formula repeats exactly (cf. 1:2 qum lech) — YHWH does not reword his command to suit the prophet's experience. The only addition: veqera eleiha et haqeriah asher anochi dover eleicha — 'and proclaim to it the proclamation that I tell you.' The content of the proclamation is withheld until it is actually delivered (v4) — the text models Jonah's compliance without giving him advance knowledge of what he will say.
3.3: vayaqam Yonah vayelech el Nineveh kidvar YHWH: 'So Jonah arose and went to Nineveh according to the word of the LORD.' The contrast with 1:3 (vayaqam Yonah livroach, he rose to flee) is exact and intentional: same verb vayaqam (rose), opposite direction. Vehineveh haita ir gedolah le'elohim mahalach sheloshet yamim — 'now Nineveh was an exceedingly great city, a three-day journey.' The unusual superlative le'elohim (to God = exceedingly, the divine elative in Hebrew) underscores the magnitude of the task. The Nineveh of Jonah's time was indeed one of the largest cities in the ancient world (confirmed by archaeology).
3.4: od arbaim yom veNineveh nehepachet: 'Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown.' This is the entire content of Jonah's proclamation — five Hebrew words. The brevity is striking for a prophetic oracle. The key word is nehepachet (from hafach, H2015) — to be overturned/overthrown — the exact verb used for Sodom's destruction (Gen 19:25; Amos 4:11). But hafach also means 'to be changed/transformed' (used of Saul's heart being changed, 1 Sam 10:6; of the heart 'turned,' Lam 1:20). The narrative's resolution: Nineveh is nehepachet — transformed by repentance rather than overthrown by judgment. The ambiguity is built into the proclamation itself.
3.5: vaya'aminu anshei Nineveh be'elohim: 'And the people of Nineveh believed in God.' The verb vaya'aminu (believed/trusted, from aman, H539) — the root of amen — marks the greatest Gentile mass repentance in Scripture. The same verb is used of Abraham's faith in Gen 15:6 (vehe'emin baYHWH, and he believed in YHWH, 'and it was counted to him as righteousness'). The response is immediate and comprehensive: from the greatest to the least (migadolam ad qetanam), they proclaimed a fast and put on sackcloth. The sackcloth (saq) is the formal garb of mourning and repentance.
3.6: vayigga hadavar el melech Nineveh: 'And the word reached the king of Nineveh.' The passive vayigga (and it reached/touched) — the word's spreading from the people to the king is natural but the formulation has an almost physical quality. The king's response is described with four verbs: he arose (vayaqam) from his throne, removed (vayyaavar) his robe, covered himself with sackcloth (vayechas saq), and sat in ashes (vayeshev al haeefer). The sequence moves from the highest status-symbol (throne) to the deepest humility-symbol (ashes) — the reversal principle that permeates the entire book.
3.7: al yitam adam uvehema baqar vetzon: 'Let neither man nor beast, herd nor flock, taste anything.' The royal decree extends the fast to include livestock — an unusual feature that underscores the totality of the communal response. The inclusion of animals in fasting (wearing sackcloth, 3:8) is not known from any other OT text but is attested in Greco-Roman accounts of Persian mourning practices. Whether this is narrative hyperbole for emphasis or a historical memory, it communicates the intensity and comprehensiveness of Nineveh's repentance: even the animals participate in the mourning that the prophet who fled to avoid this very event had refused to facilitate.
3.8: veyikasu saqim ha'adam uvehema: 'But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth.' The decree goes beyond fasting to active moral turning: veyashuvu ish miderko hara'ah umin hechamah asher bekhapeihim — 'let everyone turn from his evil way and from the violence that is in his hands.' The two terms derekh ra'ah (evil way) and chamas (violence, H2555) are the two charges leveled against the pre-flood generation (Gen 6:5, 11) — Nineveh's repentance is using the language of the most comprehensive wickedness in biblical memory, suggesting the gravity of the sin being acknowledged.
3.9: mi yodea yashuv venicham ha'elohim: 'Who knows? God may turn and relent.' The king's theological formulation is remarkable: mi yodea (who knows) — not presumption but humble openness to divine mercy. The same phrase appears in Joel 2:14 (in the same repentance context) and 2 Sam 12:22 (David hoping for the child's life). Yashuv venicham (turn and relent) — nicham (H5162) is the verb for divine relenting/repenting/changing course, used in Exod 32:12-14 and Joel 2:13-14. The king uses the exact vocabulary of the prophetic tradition about divine relenting — how he knew this vocabulary is unexplained, which is part of Jonah's theological mystery.
3.10: vayar ha'elohim et maaseihem ki shavu miderech ra'atam: 'When God saw what they did, how they turned from their evil way, God relented of the disaster.' The sequence mirrors Exod 32:14 and Joel 2:13-14: God sees the repentance and vayinachem (relented). The crucial word: velo asah — 'and he did not do it.' The disaster he had threatened did not come. This is the theological center of the book: YHWH is genuinely responsive to repentance — even Gentile repentance, even Assyrian repentance. The character formula of Exod 34:6-7 (slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love) is at work, as Jonah himself will confirm in his complaint (4:2).

4.1: vayera el Yonah raah gedolah vayichar lo: 'But it displeased Jonah exceedingly, and he was angry.' The ironic wordplay is at its sharpest: in 1:2, Nineveh's ra'ah (wickedness/evil) had come up before YHWH; now YHWH's forgiveness of Nineveh is raah gedolah (great evil) to Jonah. The same evaluative word applied to the city's sin is now applied to Jonah's reaction to the city's salvation. Vayichar lo (he was angry, literally 'it burned for him') — the verb charah (H2734) will be used four times in ch4 (vv1, 4, 9[×2]) building the chapter's emotional arc.
4.2: haloa zeh devari ad heyoti al admati: 'Is this not what I said when I was still in my country?' Jonah's prayer of complaint contains the book's theological heart: he quotes Exod 34:6 — ki ata el channun verachum erech apayim verab chesed venicham al hara'ah — 'for I knew that you are a gracious God and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting from disaster.' The six-fold character formula from Exod 34:6-7 (the most quoted verse in the OT) is here used as an accusation: Jonah's complaint is not ignorance of God's character but an objection to that character being extended to Assyria. He did not flee out of fear of Nineveh but out of a desire that they not repent and be saved.
4.3: veatta YHWH qach na et nafshi mimmeni: 'Therefore now, O LORD, please take my life from me.' Jonah's death-wish echoes Elijah's in 1 Kgs 19:4 (qach nafshi — same phrase) — the prophet burned out and wanting to die after his mission. But the contexts differ: Elijah is exhausted after a great victory and terrified by Jezebel's threat; Jonah is angry because his mission succeeded in a way he did not want. Ki tov moti mechayai — 'for death is better for me than life.' This is the book's lowest point for Jonah: the prophet who has just experienced the miracle of Nineveh's repentance requests death rather than endorses it.
4.4: vaYHWH ha'tov charah lach: 'And the LORD said, Do you do well to be angry?' YHWH's response to Jonah's complaint is a single question: ha'tov charah lach — literally 'Is it good for you to burn/be angry?' The question is deceptively simple but theologically pointed: is your anger the right response? The word tov (good) introduces the book's second evaluative frame — the first was ra'ah (evil/bad), now tov (good). YHWH does not rebuke Jonah's anger outright but poses the question, allowing Jonah to answer it through his own subsequent behavior. The question is left unanswered at this point — Jonah simply walks out of the city.
4.5: vayetze Yonah min hair vayeshev miqqedem lair: 'Jonah went out of the city and sat to the east of the city.' Jonah, receiving no satisfying answer to his complaint, does what displaced prophets do: he waits. He makes a booth (sukkah, H5521) and sits in its shade to see what will happen to the city. The sukkah vocabulary evokes the Feast of Tabernacles/Booths — the harvest festival celebrating YHWH's provision in the wilderness and the nations coming to Jerusalem (Zech 14:16-19 connects this festival with Gentile nations, including, specifically, Egypt and Nineveh). Jonah under his sukkah at Nineveh is a bitterly ironic Tabernacles scene.
4.6: vayeman YHWH elohim qiqayon: 'Now the LORD God appointed a plant.' The same verb mana (appointed, H4487) as 1:17 (the fish). YHWH now appoints four objects: fish (1:17), plant (4:6), worm (4:7), east wind (4:8) — all four are instruments of his pedagogical purpose for Jonah. The qiqayon (H7021, usually translated gourd or castor oil plant — a large-leafed plant that provides shade quickly) grows over Jonah's head to give shade. Wayesamach Yonah al haqiqayon simcha gedolah — 'and Jonah was exceedingly glad because of the plant, with great joy.' The same adjectival construction as 1:16 (the sailors' great fear before YHWH) — Jonah's intense joy over the plant stands in ironic contrast to his reaction to Nineveh's salvation.
4.7: vayeman ha'elohim tola'at baalot hashachar lamochorat: 'But God appointed a worm when dawn came up the next day, and it attacked the plant, so that it withered.' The worm (tola'at, H8438) — ordinarily a symbol of death and decay (Job 24:20; Isa 14:11; 66:24) — is YHWH's instrument to remove Jonah's joy. Vatach (attacked/struck) — the same verb used of snakebite and scorpion sting. The plant that provided abundant shade is dried up by a single worm — the reversal of Jonah's brief comfort is as complete as it is sudden. This precision — a single worm, at dawn, the very next day — underscores that nothing in this narrative is accidental.
4.8: vayehi bizerot hashemesh vayeman elohim ruach qadim charish: 'When the sun rose, God appointed a scorching east wind.' The qadim charish (scorching east wind) is the sirocco — the hot, desiccating wind from the desert that can raise temperatures dramatically and sap a person's strength. Under this combined assault (sun and wind without shade), Jonah asks to die a second time: tov moti mechayai — 'death is better for me than life' (verbatim repetition of 4:3). The doubling of the death-wish marks the depth of his disorder: first he wanted to die because Nineveh was saved; now he wants to die because a plant died. The disproportionality is the book's indictment.
4.9: vaYHWH el Yonah ha'tov charah lach al haqiqayon: 'Then God said to Jonah, Do you do well to be angry for the plant?' YHWH repeats his question from v4 but specifies the object: the plant. Jonah's answer is unambiguous: heitev charah li ad mawet — 'I do well to be angry, angry enough to die.' The escalation from v4 (where Jonah had silently walked away) to v9 (where he asserts the rightness of his anger to the point of death) marks Jonah's refusal to be corrected. The book's final pedagogical moment is set: YHWH will use Jonah's own emotional investment in the plant to expose the incoherence of his position.
4.10: atta chasta al haqiqayon asher lo amalta bo: 'You pity the plant, for which you did not labor.' YHWH's argument moves from lesser to greater: you (atta, emphatic) had compassion (chasta, H2347 — to spare/take care of/pity) for a plant that you did not grow, tend, or cultivate, that came up in a night and perished in a night. The plant is YHWH's miniature case study for the principle of care without investment. The argument is not just rational but emotional: Jonah's emotional response to the plant's death reveals that he is capable of caring deeply about things — his care is just misdirected.
4.11: va'ani lo achusa al Nineveh hair hagedolah: 'And should I not pity Nineveh, that great city?' The book ends with a question — YHWH's unanswered rhetorical question to Jonah and, through the narrative, to every reader. Nineveh has harbeh mish-shteim esreh riboh adam — more than 120,000 persons who do not know their right hand from their left (asher lo yada bein yemino lismolo — the idiom for moral ignorance/infancy, not literally unable to distinguish directions) — plus much cattle (ubehema rabbah). The book's final word is rabbah (much/many/great) — the same adjective used of the great wind (1:4), the great storm (1:12), and Jonah's great joy at the plant (4:6). YHWH's compassion for Nineveh is proportioned to its greatness and ignorance; Jonah's pitilessness is exposed as incoherent in light of his pity for a plant. The deliberate open ending invites the reader to answer for Jonah — and for themselves.
